danger_signals.visual_form: Large muscular figure chasing player character across platform
danger_signals.meaning: Primary threat; collision results in loss of life

danger_signals.visual_form: Projectiles descending from top of screen toward player
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazards that deal damage on contact

danger_signals.visual_form: Superimposed figure in top-right area of level (NES version)
Objects (hearts, musical notes, letters) remaining on-screen past timeout threshold
danger_signals.meaning: Time pressure mechanic; failure to collect before despawn results in life loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Animated chasing behavior across multiple level tiers
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat state; Brutus can traverse down levels and jump upward to attack

safety_signals.visual_form: Single can of spinach visible on each level
safety_signals.meaning: Collectible that grants temporary invincibility and allows counter-attack against Brutus

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual effect indicating Popeye's temporary invulnerability
safety_signals.meaning: Brief window where Brutus flees and player can eliminate threat via contact

safety_signals.visual_form: Bag object on first screen of each cycle with bucket suspended above
safety_signals.meaning: Optional stun mechanic; hitting bag drops bucket to temporarily incapacitate Brutus for score bonus

safety_signals.visual_form: Figure at top of screen dropping collectible items
safety_signals.meaning: Non-hostile entity providing resources; represents objective source

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual counter for collected items (hearts/notes/letters) per level
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward level completion; safety correlates with collection progress

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Occupies platform structures; operates on stairs and ladders

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Muscular antagonist figure, distinctly larger frame than player
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Normal chase state vs. stunned state (bucket collision) vs. fleeing state (post-spinach)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mobile across all platform levels; pursues Popeye aggressively

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Female figure positioned at top of screen
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fixed position source; generates level objectives

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Witch character superimposed on level area
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Top-right level position (NES version); alternative threat to Brutus

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Brutus displayed in active pursuit animation; weapons visible on-screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Popeye can only defend via punching projectiles; cannot damage Brutus; must evade

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Popeye visually appears invulnerable; Brutus animation shifts to fleeing behavior
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Brutus runs away; contact with Brutus knocks him out temporarily; doubled item point values active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Brutus stunned animation; temporary immobility visual state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Score bonus awarded; Brutus remains stationary for several seconds before resuming pursuit

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dropped objects visible on lower platform level; visual urgency through positioning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must navigate to item before despawn; failure triggers life loss

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Required item count collected (24 hearts / 16 notes / 24 letters HELP depending on level)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression to next screen cycle; difficulty escalation through layout variation

environmental_storytelling.example: Olive Oyl positioned at top of screen as objective source; Popeye at middle platform tiers; Brutus pursuing across all accessible levels creates visual narrative of pursuit and resource acquisition under pressure

Screen 1 features hearts (love/survival theme), Screen 2 features musical notes (entertainment/character), Screen 3 features HELP letters (distress/character vulnerability) — visual thematic progression across arcade cycle
Punching bag on first screen with suspended bucket indicates Popeye's combat capability and environmental mastery; conveyed through interactive object design rather than exposition
Brutus rendered visually larger than Popeye, creating immediate visual communication of power differential; spinach acts as temporary equalizer through invincibility visual state

Platform layout forces pursuit into specific pathways; Brutus mobility limitations (must use stairs/ladders like Popeye) creates cat-and-mouse dynamic; no jump mechanic for player emphasizes navigation skill over reflex jumping

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Chases Popeye across platform tiers; can traverse down to lower levels, reach sideways to adjacent tiers, and jump upward to strike player positioned above
Temporarily immobilized; cannot pursue; remains stationary for several seconds before resuming normal behavior
Runs away from Popeye; can be temporarily knocked out via direct contact; returns to normal pursuit after invincibility window expires
Can punch projectiles to destroy them; walks/climbs freely on platforms; cannot damage Brutus without spinach; loses life on contact with threats
Temporary invulnerability to all threats; can defeat Brutus through contact; item collection point value doubled; behavior reverts after time limit
Descend from top of screen toward player; can be destroyed by Popeye's punch attack; deal damage on contact if not destroyed
Remains at top-screen position; generates collectible objects (hearts/notes/letters) that Popeye must retrieve; non-interactive but goal-related
Acts as secondary threat requiring avoidance; positioned superimposed on level area (NES version); exact interaction mechanics less documented than Brutus threat

notes: Popeye NES (1986) is a direct port of the 1982 arcade game with minimal visual divergence. Key visual communication relies on character animation states and spatial positioning rather than color coding or complex visual effects (platform limitations). The unused skull graphics noted in TCRF suggest the NES version simplified the Sea Hag visual presentation compared to arcade (removed skull platform stack). The game's core danger/safety communication emerges through behavioral animation changes (Brutus pursuit vs. fleeing) rather than palette swaps. No faction-based visual conflict beyond hero-vs-antagonist binary; world-state changes manifest primarily through collectible presence/absence and enemy behavioral shifts.
